title: Exhaustive Optimisation of Automorphism Groups for Stabiliser Codes
summary: An important measure of utility for a quantum code is the identification of which logical operations can be implemented fault-tolerantly on its codespace. We introduce a framework which leverages the automorphism groups of associated classical codes, the choice of logical basis and exploitation of code equivalence to construct all distinct implementable realisations of each valid logical operation for a given $[[n,k,d]]$ code. We establish conjugacy classes and group transversals (unrelated to transversality) as key explanatory concepts. We subsequently motivate and calculate two figures-of-merit that can be optimised with this framework. Our results yield a table of optimal logical operations and their corresponding physical circuits for all small stabiliser codes with $n \leq 7$ and $k \leq 2$, drawn from quantum databases. This exhaustive table of results provides the optimal physical implementations of logical operations which may be advantageous for both magic state cultivation and experimental purposes.

AI.tldr: Framework for constructing all distinct fault-tolerant logical operations on quantum codes using classical code automorphism groups, logical basis choice, and code equivalence, with optimization metrics and exhaustive results for small stabilizer codes.
AI.motivation: To systematically identify and implement all fault-tolerant logical operations on quantum codes, which is crucial for quantum error correction and practical quantum computing applications like magic state cultivation and experimental implementations.
AI.method: Leverages automorphism groups of associated classical codes, choice of logical basis, and exploitation of code equivalence to construct all distinct implementable realizations of each valid logical operation. Uses conjugacy classes and group transversals as key explanatory concepts.
AI.result: Developed two figures-of-merit that can be optimized with the framework. Produced exhaustive table of optimal logical operations and corresponding physical circuits for all small stabilizer codes with n ≤ 7 and k ≤ 2, drawn from quantum databases.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides optimal physical implementations of logical operations that are advantageous for both magic state cultivation and experimental purposes, offering a systematic approach to fault-tolerant quantum computation.

title: Temperature and integrability-breaking correspondence via adiabatic transformations
summary: We reveal a correspondence between temperature and integrability-breaking in classical and quantum many-body systems through the lens of geometry and adiabatic transformations. Decreasing the temperature, obtained in a standard way through the derivative of entropy with respect to energy, steers the system towards an integrable point despite strong integrability-breaking interactions. Auto-correlation functions of local observables exhibit slow relaxation dynamics, which violates ergodicity on the approach to this integrable point. Subsequently, the average fidelity susceptibility of stationary states satisfies scaling relations near the integrable point, in close analogy with continuous phase transitions. We further find that the dynamical exponent encompassing relaxation can be different in the quantum and classical models, depending on dimension of the systems. Collectively, our results establish temperature as a tunable control parameter for chaos and puts it on equal footing with integrability-breaking perturbations.

AI.tldr: Temperature acts as a control parameter for chaos, with decreasing temperature steering systems toward integrable points despite integrability-breaking interactions, revealing temperature-integrable correspondence.
AI.motivation: To establish a fundamental correspondence between temperature and integrability-breaking in many-body systems, revealing temperature as a tunable control parameter for chaos on par with integrability-breaking perturbations.
AI.method: Using geometric and adiabatic transformation approaches to analyze classical and quantum many-body systems, examining auto-correlation functions, relaxation dynamics, and fidelity susceptibility scaling near integrable points.
AI.result: Decreasing temperature drives systems toward integrable points despite strong integrability-breaking interactions, with slow relaxation dynamics violating ergodicity. Fidelity susceptibility shows scaling relations analogous to continuous phase transitions, with dynamical exponents differing between quantum and classical models depending on dimension.
AI.conclusion: Temperature serves as a tunable control parameter for chaos, establishing it on equal footing with integrability-breaking perturbations and revealing a fundamental temperature-integrable correspondence in many-body systems.

title: Constructing Fermionic Dynamics with Closed Moment Hierarchies
summary: We construct a broad class of completely positive maps and Go\-rini--Kossakowski--Sudarshan-Lindblad generators for fermionic systems induced by linear transformations of system and environment modes. For these maps, we derive explicit Heisenberg-picture formulas for arbitrary normally ordered monomials in terms of minors of the underlying mode-transformation matrices and environment correlation tensors. We show that for even environment states the linear span of monomials up to any fixed order is invariant, which yields closed equations for low-order moments and makes their computation efficient. We also discuss the relation of this construction to second quantization of non-Hermitian one-particle contractions and extend the formalism to completely positive maps arising from post-selection.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a mathematical framework for constructing completely positive maps and GKSL generators for fermionic systems using linear transformations of system and environment modes, providing explicit formulas for normally ordered monomials and efficient computation of low-order moments.
AI.motivation: To establish a systematic construction method for completely positive maps and GKSL generators in fermionic open quantum systems that enables efficient computation of correlation functions and low-order moments through explicit formulas.
AI.method: Constructs completely positive maps induced by linear transformations of system and environment modes, derives Heisenberg-picture formulas for arbitrary normally ordered monomials using minors of mode-transformation matrices and environment correlation tensors, and demonstrates invariance of monomial spans for even environment states.
AI.result: Obtains explicit formulas for correlation functions in terms of minors, shows that linear spans of monomials up to fixed order are invariant under the maps (enabling closed equations for low-order moments), relates construction to second quantization of non-Hermitian one-particle contractions, and extends formalism to post-selection scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an efficient computational tool for fermionic open quantum systems with applications to correlation function calculations, low-order moment analysis, and extensions to post-selection scenarios, connecting to fundamental concepts in quantum dynamics.

title: The topological gap at criticality: scaling exponent d + η, universality, and scope
summary: The topological gap $Δ= TP_{H_1}^{real} - TP_{H_1}^{shuf}$ -- the excess $H_1$ total persistence of the majority-spin alpha complex over a density-matched null -- encodes critical correlations in spin models. We establish finite-size scaling: $Δ(L,T) = A L^{d+η} G_-(L|t/T_c|)$, with $G_-(x) \sim (1+x/x_0)^{-(1+β/ν)}$. For 2D Ising, $α= 2.249 \pm 0.038$, matching $d+η= 9/4$ to $0.03σ$; the $G_-$ exponent $γ= 1.089 \pm 0.077$ is consistent with $1+β/ν= 9/8$ ($ΔR^2 < 10^{-5}$). For 2D Potts $q=3$ with $L$ up to 1024, $α= 2.272 \pm 0.024$ ($0.2σ$ from $d+η= 2.267$), with two-term corrections to scaling ($R^2 = 0.9999$). The $G_-$ exponent $γ= 1.114$ (68% CI $[1.053, 1.173]$) matches $1+β/ν= 17/15$. Scope boundaries: the law fails for 2D Potts $q=4$ ($α= 2.347 \pm 0.017$, $9.3σ$ from $d+η= 5/2$) where logarithmic corrections prevent convergence, and for raw 3D Ising ($4σ$ from $d+η$), but density normalization $Δ/|M|^{1/2}$ recovers $α= 3.06 \pm 0.04$ ($0.6σ$). The framework fails for first-order, BKT, and percolation. The criterion: $α= d+η$ holds when corrections to scaling are algebraic ($ω> 0$) but fails when logarithmic ($ω\to 0$).

AI.tldr: Topological gap Δ shows universal finite-size scaling Δ(L,T) = A L^{d+η} G_-(L|t/T_c|) for critical spin models with algebraic corrections, matching exact exponents for 2D Ising and Potts q=3, but fails for models with logarithmic corrections (Potts q=4, 3D Ising without normalization) or non-algebraic transitions.
AI.motivation: To establish a universal finite-size scaling law for the topological gap Δ (excess H₁ total persistence) in critical spin models, and determine when this scaling holds versus when it fails due to different types of corrections to scaling.
AI.method: Analyze finite-size scaling of topological gap Δ = TP_{H₁}^{real} - TP_{H₁}^{shuf} using scaling form Δ(L,T) = A L^{d+η} G_-(L|t/T_c|) with G_-(x) ∼ (1+x/x₀)^{-(1+β/ν)}. Test on 2D Ising, 2D Potts q=3, 2D Potts q=4, and 3D Ising models with system sizes up to L=1024.
AI.result: For 2D Ising: α = 2.249 ± 0.038 matches d+η = 9/4 (0.03σ); G_- exponent γ = 1.089 ± 0.077 matches 1+β/ν = 9/8. For 2D Potts q=3: α = 2.272 ± 0.024 (0.2σ from d+η = 2.267); γ = 1.114 matches 1+β/ν = 17/15. For 2D Potts q=4: α = 2.347 ± 0.017 (9.3σ from d+η = 5/2) due to logarithmic corrections. For 3D Ising: raw Δ gives 4σ deviation, but density-normalized Δ/|M|^{1/2} recovers α = 3.06 ± 0.04 (0.6σ from d+η).
AI.conclusion: The scaling law α = d+η holds for critical spin models with algebraic corrections to scaling (ω > 0), as demonstrated for 2D Ising and Potts q=3, but fails for models with logarithmic corrections (ω → 0) like Potts q=4, and for first-order, BKT, and percolation transitions. Density normalization can recover scaling in some cases like 3D Ising.

title: Programmable recirculating bricks mesh architecture for quantum photonics
summary: General-purpose programmable photonic processors offer a flexible foundation for integrating various functionalities within a single chip. A two-dimensional hexagonal waveguide mesh of Mach Zehnder interferometers has been shown to have great potential in the field of microwave photonics. Additionally, they are a promising platform for the creation of unitary linear transformations, which are key elements in photonic neural networks, In this article, we expand the portfolio of available applications for recirculating bricks mesh architecture to quantum technologies. We will show that a single programmable optical system is capable of performing various functions depending on the requirements. In particular, we will focus in this work on boson sampling, a task that best demonstrates quantum advantage, as well as on tasks that enable the determination of photon indistinguishability, which plays a key role in photonic quantum technologies. We will also show that, in addition to spatial modes, the same optical system can be equally well-suited for work on temporal modes through the implementation of an appropriate number of loops.

AI.tldr: Programmable photonic processors using hexagonal waveguide meshes can be extended from classical applications to quantum technologies, enabling boson sampling and photon indistinguishability measurements through spatial and temporal mode manipulation.
AI.motivation: To expand the application portfolio of recirculating bricks mesh architecture from classical microwave photonics and photonic neural networks to quantum technologies, demonstrating that a single programmable optical system can perform multiple quantum functions.
AI.method: Utilizes a two-dimensional hexagonal waveguide mesh of Mach-Zehnder interferometers as a programmable photonic processor, extending its capabilities to quantum applications by implementing appropriate configurations for spatial and temporal modes.
AI.result: The system demonstrates capability to perform boson sampling (a quantum advantage benchmark) and tasks for determining photon indistinguishability, with equal suitability for both spatial and temporal modes through loop implementation.
AI.conclusion: A single programmable photonic processor based on hexagonal waveguide meshes can serve multiple quantum technology applications, including boson sampling and photon characterization, bridging classical and quantum photonic functionalities.

title: Anomalous scaling in redirection networks
summary: In networks that grow by isotropic redirection (IR), a new node selects an initial target node uniformly at random and attaches to a randomly chosen neighbor of the target. The emerging networks exhibit leaf proliferation, in which the number of nonleaves scales sublinearly as $N^μ$ and the degree distribution has an algebraic tail with exponent $1+μ$. To understand these mysterious properties, we introduce a class of models with redirection to leaves whenever possible. The resulting networks exhibit qualitatively similar phenomenology to IR networks, but avoid the inherent non-locality of the IR growth rule. These networks admit an analytical description of the leaf degree distribution, from which we extract the exponent $μ$.

AI.tldr: Networks grown by isotropic redirection (IR) show leaf proliferation with sublinear scaling of nonleaves and algebraic degree distribution. A new class of models redirecting to leaves when possible reproduces similar phenomenology while avoiding IR's non-locality, enabling analytical derivation of the leaf degree distribution and exponent μ.
AI.motivation: To understand the mysterious properties of networks grown by isotropic redirection (IR), particularly leaf proliferation where the number of nonleaves scales sublinearly as N^μ and the degree distribution has an algebraic tail with exponent 1+μ.
AI.method: Introduce a new class of network growth models with redirection to leaves whenever possible, which avoids the inherent non-locality of the IR growth rule while maintaining qualitatively similar phenomenology.
AI.result: The new models exhibit leaf proliferation similar to IR networks but are analytically tractable, allowing derivation of the leaf degree distribution and extraction of the exponent μ that characterizes the sublinear scaling of nonleaves.
AI.conclusion: By introducing models with redirection to leaves when possible, the paper provides analytical understanding of the mysterious properties observed in isotropic redirection networks, particularly the leaf proliferation phenomenon and the algebraic degree distribution exponent.

title: Resource Estimation via Efficient Compilation of Key Quantum Primitives
summary: Resource estimation is a significant challenge in evaluating fault tolerant quantum computers. Existing approaches often rely on either fixed architectural assumptions or coarse analytical models that fail to capture the interaction between hardware constraints and circuit compilation. This challenge is particularly acute for neutral atom quantum computers, where architectural features such as atom movement, measurement zones, and multi-species arrays introduce a broad design space for implementing fault tolerant computation. Addressing the need for a tighter feedback loop between hardware design and practical application development, we present a compilation-driven framework for quantum resource estimation that translates arbitrary quantum circuits into logical primitive operations with known physical resource costs. This framework allows for easily configurable hardware assumptions that enable rapid comparison of different architectural design choices. We apply our approach to two early fault tolerant quantum simulation and optimization workloads, assuming the use of the surface code, revealing several architectural trends. While the production of magic states continues to be the dominant source of overhead for these benchmarks, access to movement can save time on cultivation and important transversal gates. As problem size grows, routing and qubit movement become dominant bottlenecks, highlighting the need for movement-aware compiler optimizations and frugal routing strategies. Finally, our results suggest that neutral atom architectures combining dual-species arrays with controlled qubit movement offer a promising path toward near-term advantage on fault tolerant devices.

AI.tldr: A compilation-driven framework for quantum resource estimation that translates quantum circuits into logical operations with known physical costs, enabling rapid comparison of architectural design choices for neutral atom quantum computers.
AI.motivation: Existing resource estimation approaches for fault tolerant quantum computers either rely on fixed architectural assumptions or coarse analytical models that fail to capture hardware-circuit interactions. This is particularly challenging for neutral atom quantum computers with unique features like atom movement, measurement zones, and multi-species arrays.
AI.method: A compilation-driven framework that translates arbitrary quantum circuits into logical primitive operations with known physical resource costs. The framework allows for easily configurable hardware assumptions to enable rapid comparison of different architectural design choices, specifically applied to neutral atom architectures with surface code error correction.
AI.result: Application to early fault tolerant quantum simulation and optimization workloads reveals several architectural trends: magic state production remains the dominant overhead source, but movement access saves time on cultivation and transversal gates. As problem size grows, routing and qubit movement become dominant bottlenecks. Dual-species arrays with controlled qubit movement show promise for near-term advantage.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides tighter feedback between hardware design and application development. Results highlight the need for movement-aware compiler optimizations and frugal routing strategies. Neutral atom architectures combining dual-species arrays with controlled qubit movement offer a promising path toward near-term advantage on fault tolerant quantum devices.

title: The Mpemba effect likes to hit a wall
summary: The historical Mpemba effect involves a first-order phase transition. This has prompted the experimental realization of microscopic proxies in the form of a colloidal particle trapped in an asymmetric double well, for which the Mpemba effect has indeed been observed. We establish that the existence of the one-dimensional Mpemba effect for a polynomial potential is driven solely by the presence of a hard enough boundary, irrespective of the potential's double-well shape. We then show that the physics of the underlying Mpemba effect is governed not only by single-well physics but also by the high-temperature initial regime.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes that the Mpemba effect in one-dimensional polynomial potentials is driven by hard boundaries rather than double-well shapes, and reveals that the effect's physics involves both single-well dynamics and high-temperature initial conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental mechanisms behind the Mpemba effect in microscopic systems, particularly whether it requires double-well potentials or can be explained by simpler boundary conditions and initial temperature regimes.
AI.method: Analysis of one-dimensional polynomial potentials, focusing on the role of hard boundaries versus double-well shapes, and examination of both single-well physics and high-temperature initial conditions in governing the Mpemba effect.
AI.result: The Mpemba effect in one-dimensional polynomial potentials is driven solely by the presence of sufficiently hard boundaries, independent of the potential's double-well shape. The effect's physics is governed by both single-well dynamics and the high-temperature initial regime.
AI.conclusion: The Mpemba effect can occur without requiring double-well potentials, with hard boundaries being the essential ingredient. This provides a simpler explanation for the effect observed in microscopic colloidal systems and clarifies the role of initial temperature conditions.

title: Quantum polymorphism characterisation of commutativity gadgets in all quantum models
summary:
Commutativity gadgets provide a technique for lifting classical reductions between constraint satisfaction problems to quantum-sound reductions between the corresponding nonlocal games. We develop a general framework for commutativity gadgets in the setting of quantum homomorphisms between finite relational structures. Building on the notion of quantum homomorphism spaces, we introduce a uniform notion of commutativity gadget capturing the finite-dimensional quantum, quantum approximate, and commuting-operator models. In the robust setting, we use the weighted-algebra formalism for approximate quantum homomorphisms to capture corresponding notions of robust commutativity gadgets.
  Our main results characterize both non-robust and robust commutativity gadgets purely in terms of quantum polymorphism spaces: in any model, existence of a commutativity gadget is equivalent to the collapse of the corresponding quantum polymorphisms to classical ones at arity $|A|^2$, and robust gadgets are characterized by stable commutativity of the appropriate weighted polymorphism algebra. We use this characterisation to show relations between the classes of commutativity gadget, notably that existence of a robust commutativity gadget is equivalent to the existence of a corresponding non-robust one.
  Finally, we prove that quantum polymorphisms of complete graphs $K_n$ have a very special structure, wherein the noncommutative behaviour only comes from the quantum permutation group $S_n^+$. Combining this with techniques from combinatorial group theory, we construct separations between commutativity-gadget classes: we exhibit a relational structure admitting a finite-dimensional commutativity gadget but no quantum approximate gadget, and, conditional on the existence of a non-hyperlinear group, a structure admitting a quantum approximate commutativity gadget but no commuting-operator gadget.

AI.tldr: Commutativity gadgets lift classical CSP reductions to quantum nonlocal games; framework connects quantum homomorphisms, polymorphism spaces, and weighted algebras to characterize gadget existence via quantum polymorphism collapse; separations between gadget classes constructed using quantum permutation groups and combinatorial group theory.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified framework for commutativity gadgets that bridges classical constraint satisfaction problem reductions with quantum-sound reductions in nonlocal games, enabling systematic study of quantum homomorphisms across different quantum models (finite-dimensional, approximate, commuting-operator).
AI.method: Introduces quantum homomorphism spaces and uniform commutativity gadget definitions capturing multiple quantum models; uses weighted-algebra formalism for robust gadgets; characterizes gadgets via quantum polymorphism spaces and their collapse to classical polymorphisms; employs combinatorial group theory and quantum permutation group structures for separations.
AI.result: Characterizes commutativity gadget existence as collapse of quantum polymorphisms to classical ones at arity |A|²; shows robust gadget existence equivalent to non-robust existence; proves quantum polymorphisms of complete graphs K_n derive only from quantum permutation group S_n⁺; constructs separations between gadget classes using group-theoretic techniques.
AI.conclusion: Commutativity gadgets provide a powerful framework connecting classical CSP reductions to quantum nonlocal games; their existence is fundamentally tied to quantum polymorphism collapse; separations between gadget classes reveal subtle distinctions between quantum models, with implications for quantum constraint satisfaction and nonlocal games.

title: Symmetry-Informed Term Filtering for Continuum Equation Discovery
summary: Discovering governing equations, whether manually or by data-driven methods, has been central in physics and related areas. Since governing equations are typically constrained by a set of symmetries, using symmetry constraints to restrict terms is usually the first step in manually formulating a governing equation, but it often becomes intractable for complex systems with high-order derivatives or multiple fields. When a data-driven method is used, on the other hand, imposing physical constraints such as symmetries typically requires manual preprocessing or computationally expensive iterative procedures. Here, we propose an algebraic filtering method that enumerates all symmetry-allowed terms for continuum equations within a finite candidate space. By treating symmetry generators as linear operators on the candidate space, we reduce the problem of enforcing both discrete and continuous symmetries to solving a set of linear kernel equations. The solution yields a provably complete list of permitted terms. We demonstrate the method's effectiveness by identifying invariant terms for systems with dihedral symmetry and recovering the governing equations for the Toner--Tu and Kardar--Parisi--Zhang systems, including higher-order terms useful for extending known models. The method provides a systematic way to obtain a symmetry-allowed search space for data-driven equation discovery, e.g., the sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics method.

AI.tldr: Algebraic filtering method that systematically enumerates all symmetry-allowed terms for continuum equations by treating symmetry generators as linear operators and solving kernel equations.
AI.motivation: Current methods for discovering governing equations either require manual symmetry analysis (intractable for complex systems) or computationally expensive iterative procedures when imposing physical constraints in data-driven approaches.
AI.method: Treat symmetry generators as linear operators on candidate space, reduce enforcement of both discrete and continuous symmetries to solving linear kernel equations, yielding provably complete list of permitted terms.
AI.result: Successfully identified invariant terms for systems with dihedral symmetry and recovered governing equations for Toner-Tu and Kardar-Parisi-Zhang systems, including higher-order terms useful for extending known models.
AI.conclusion: Provides systematic way to obtain symmetry-allowed search space for data-driven equation discovery, enabling more efficient and complete exploration of physically valid governing equations.

title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
summary: This paper develops a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations. For a linear system of the form $Ax=b$, the large square matrix $A$ is partitioned into smaller square block submatrices, each of which is known only to a single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computer. Each NISQ computer communicates with certain other quantum computers in the same row and column of the block partition, where the communication patterns are described by the row- and column-neighbor graphs, both of which are connected. The proposed algorithm integrates a variant of the variational quantum linear solver at each computer with distributed classical optimization techniques. The derivation of the quantum cost function provides insight into the design of the distributed algorithm. Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate that the proposed distributed quantum algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers and is therefore not limited by the capacity of a single quantum computer.

AI.tldr: Distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations by partitioning matrix across multiple NISQ computers with coordinated optimization.
AI.motivation: To overcome the capacity limitations of single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computers when solving large-scale linear systems, enabling solution of problems whose size scales with the number of available quantum computers.
AI.method: Partitions large matrix A into smaller square block submatrices distributed across multiple NISQ computers; each computer communicates with row- and column-neighbors via connected graphs; integrates variational quantum linear solver variants at each node with distributed classical optimization techniques.
AI.result: Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate the algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers, overcoming single quantum computer capacity limitations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distributed variational quantum algorithm enables solving large-scale linear equations by leveraging multiple NISQ computers with coordinated communication and optimization, providing a scalable approach beyond single quantum computer limitations.

title: Invariant measures of exclusion processes with a look-ahead rule
summary: We study a one-dimensional exclusion process with a fixed jump length $I \ge 1$ in which a particle may advance or retreat $I$ sites provided all intermediate sites are vacant, with hopping rates of Arrhenius type depending on the local headway. We identify the class of rates admitting an explicit Ising-Gibbs invariant measure, with stationarity governed by pairwise balance rather than detailed balance. In the thermodynamic limit, we derive a closed-form stationary current that recovers the mean-field prediction for look-ahead traffic flow models exactly when particles are uncorrelated, and quantifies the correlation-induced correction for non-trivial interactions, illustrated with two explicit families of interaction potentials.

AI.tldr: One-dimensional exclusion process with fixed jump length I≥1, where particles can advance or retreat I sites if intermediate sites are empty, using Arrhenius-type hopping rates based on local headway. The paper identifies rate classes admitting explicit Ising-Gibbs invariant measures governed by pairwise balance, derives closed-form stationary current in thermodynamic limit, and shows mean-field traffic flow predictions hold exactly when particles are uncorrelated.
AI.motivation: To understand traffic flow models with look-ahead interactions, where particles can make long jumps based on local conditions, and to characterize the stationary behavior of such exclusion processes with non-detailed balance dynamics.
AI.method: Study one-dimensional exclusion process with fixed jump length I, Arrhenius-type hopping rates dependent on local headway. Identify rate classes admitting explicit Ising-Gibbs invariant measures governed by pairwise balance rather than detailed balance. Analyze thermodynamic limit to derive closed-form stationary current.
AI.result: Identified specific classes of hopping rates that yield explicit Ising-Gibbs invariant measures. Derived closed-form expression for stationary current in thermodynamic limit. Showed mean-field prediction for look-ahead traffic flow models holds exactly when particles are uncorrelated, and quantified correlation-induced corrections for non-trivial interactions. Illustrated results with two explicit families of interaction potentials.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a connection between exclusion processes with long jumps and Ising-Gibbs measures through pairwise balance, provides exact stationary current formulas, and demonstrates how correlations affect traffic flow predictions in look-ahead models, offering analytical tools for studying interacting particle systems with non-detailed balance dynamics.

title: Classical shadows with arbitrary group representations
summary: Classical shadows (CS) has recently emerged as an important framework to efficiently predict properties of an unknown quantum state. A common strategy in CS protocols is to parametrize the basis in which one measures the state by a random group action; many examples of this have been proposed and studied on a case-by-case basis. In this work, we present a unified theory that allows us to simultaneously understand CS protocols based on sampling from general group representations, extending previous approaches that worked in simplified (multiplicity-free) settings. We identify a class of measurement bases which we call "centralizing bases" that allows us to analytically characterize and invert the measurement channel, minimizing classical post-processing costs. We complement this analysis by deriving general bounds on the sample-complexity necessary to obtain estimates of a given precision. Beyond its unification of previous CS protocols, our method allows us to readily generate new protocols based on other groups, or different representations of previously considered ones. For example, we characterize novel shadow protocols based on sampling from the spin and tensor representations of $\textsf{SU}(2)$, symmetric and orthogonal groups, and the exceptional Lie group $G_2$.

AI.tldr: A unified theory for classical shadows protocols based on general group representations, extending beyond multiplicity-free settings to include centralizing bases that simplify measurement channel inversion and provide sample complexity bounds.
AI.motivation: Classical shadows protocols often use random group actions to parametrize measurement bases, but existing approaches have been studied case-by-case in simplified (multiplicity-free) settings. There's a need for a unified theory that works for general group representations.
AI.method: Develop a unified framework for classical shadows protocols based on sampling from general group representations. Identify "centralizing bases" that allow analytical characterization and inversion of the measurement channel. Derive general bounds on sample complexity for given precision.
AI.result: The theory unifies previous CS protocols and enables generation of new protocols based on various groups/representations. Novel shadow protocols are characterized for spin/tensor representations of SU(2), symmetric/orthogonal groups, and exceptional Lie group G₂.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a comprehensive framework extending classical shadows beyond multiplicity-free settings, offering analytical tools for measurement channel inversion and sample complexity analysis while enabling new protocol development across diverse group representations.

title: Beyond dynamic scaling: rare events break universality
comment: Submitted; 8 pages and 10 figures (main text and Appendix)
summary: Surface growth driven by non-monomeric deposition has remained largely unexplored. We investigate a model based on the deposition of blobs with a power-law size distribution $P(s)\sim s^{-τ}$. We find that the critical exponents vary continuously with $τ$, recovering Kardar--Parisi--Zhang behavior only for $τ\ge 3$. For $τ<3$, roughness scaling exhibits strong corrections and scale invariance breaks down. We show that this behavior originates from the emergence of a second dynamical length scale $ζ$, corresponding to the linear size of the largest cluster, in addition to the usual correlation length $ξ$. The coexistence of these two relevant scales signals the breakdown of the usual Family--Vicsek scaling. These results point to a new phenomenology of surface growth beyond the standard scale-invariant paradigm.

AI.tldr: Surface growth with non-monomeric blob deposition shows continuous variation of critical exponents with blob size distribution exponent τ, with KPZ behavior only for τ≥3 and breakdown of scale invariance for τ<3 due to emergence of second dynamical length scale.
AI.motivation: To investigate surface growth phenomena driven by non-monomeric deposition, which has remained largely unexplored compared to monomeric deposition models, and to understand how power-law distributed blob sizes affect scaling behavior and universality.
AI.method: Develop a model based on deposition of blobs with power-law size distribution P(s)∼s^{-τ}, analyze scaling behavior through critical exponents, and examine the emergence of multiple length scales using correlation length ξ and cluster size ζ.
AI.result: Critical exponents vary continuously with τ, with Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) behavior recovered only for τ≥3. For τ<3, roughness scaling exhibits strong corrections and scale invariance breaks down due to emergence of second dynamical length scale ζ (linear size of largest cluster) coexisting with correlation length ξ.
AI.conclusion: Non-monomeric deposition with power-law blob sizes leads to new phenomenology beyond standard scale-invariant paradigm, characterized by continuous variation of exponents and breakdown of Family-Vicsek scaling due to multiple relevant length scales.

title: Twisted Fiber Bundle Codes over Group Algebras
summary: We introduce a twisted fiber-bundle construction of quantum CSS codes over group algebras \(R=\mathbb F_2[G]\), where each base generator carries a generator-dependent \(R\)-linear fiber twist satisfying a flatness condition. This construction extends the untwisted lifted product code, recovered when all twists are identities. We show that invertible twists (satisfying a flatness condition) give a complex chain-isomorphic to the untwisted one, so the resulting binary CSS codes have the same blocklength \(n\) and encoded dimension \(k\). In contrast, singular chain-compatible twists can lower boundary ranks and increase the number of logical qubits. Examples over \(R=\mathbb F_2[D_3]\) show that the twisted fiber bundle code can outperform the corresponding untwisted lifted-product code in \(k\) while keeping the same \(n\) and, in our examples, the same minimum distance \(d\).

AI.tldr: Twisted fiber-bundle construction of quantum CSS codes over group algebras that extends untwisted lifted product codes, with invertible twists preserving parameters and singular twists potentially increasing logical qubits.
AI.motivation: To develop a more general quantum CSS code construction that extends the untwisted lifted product code framework by incorporating generator-dependent fiber twists, potentially enabling better code parameters (increased logical qubits) while maintaining blocklength.
AI.method: Construct quantum CSS codes over group algebras R = 𝔽₂[G] using a twisted fiber-bundle approach where each base generator carries a generator-dependent R-linear fiber twist satisfying a flatness condition. The construction generalizes untwisted lifted product codes (recovered when all twists are identities).
AI.result: Invertible twists (with flatness condition) yield chain complexes isomorphic to untwisted ones, preserving blocklength n and encoded dimension k. Singular chain-compatible twists can lower boundary ranks and increase logical qubits. Examples over R = 𝔽₂[D₃] show twisted codes can outperform untwisted lifted-product codes in k while maintaining same n and minimum distance d.
AI.conclusion: The twisted fiber-bundle construction provides a flexible framework for quantum CSS codes that can potentially improve code parameters compared to untwisted lifted product codes, with singular twists offering a mechanism to increase logical qubits without changing blocklength.

title: Universal features of nonequilibrium Ising models in contact with two thermal reservoirs
summary: We derive generic properties of nonequilibrium phase transitions in all-to-all Ising models placed in contact with two thermal reservoirs, in which parameters (temperatures, interactions and field parameters) assume arbitrary values depending on the contact with each thermal bath. The presence of different kinds of external parameters leads to remarkably different sort of phase transitions. While continuous, discontinuous and even tricritical points are presented when external parameters are symmetric (e.g. the case of energetic barriers or different couplings between the system and thermal baths), the tricriticality is absent when external parameters are antisymmetric (e.g. the case of magnetic fields or biased drivings) implying that solely critical or discontinuous are possible. In such latter case, the probability distribution acquires the Boltzmann-Gibbs like form, irrespectively the model parameters when the switching between thermal reservoirs is sufficiently fast. Our work sheds light about the differences between equilibrium and nonequilibrium ingredients and theirs consequences upon phase transitions.

AI.tldr: Nonequilibrium phase transitions in all-to-all Ising models with two thermal reservoirs show different transition types depending on parameter symmetry: symmetric parameters yield continuous, discontinuous, and tricritical points, while antisymmetric parameters yield only critical or discontinuous transitions without tricriticality.
AI.motivation: To understand how different types of external parameters (symmetric vs antisymmetric) affect phase transitions in nonequilibrium systems, particularly examining differences between equilibrium and nonequilibrium ingredients and their consequences on phase transitions.
AI.method: Analysis of all-to-all Ising models coupled to two thermal reservoirs with arbitrary parameter values (temperatures, interactions, field parameters) dependent on contact with each thermal bath, examining both symmetric and antisymmetric parameter configurations.
AI.result: Symmetric parameters (energetic barriers, different couplings) produce continuous, discontinuous, and tricritical points. Antisymmetric parameters (magnetic fields, biased drivings) yield only critical or discontinuous transitions without tricriticality. For fast switching between reservoirs with antisymmetric parameters, probability distribution becomes Boltzmann-Gibbs-like regardless of model parameters.
AI.conclusion: The symmetry of external parameters fundamentally determines the types of phase transitions possible in nonequilibrium systems, highlighting key differences between equilibrium and nonequilibrium ingredients and their effects on phase transitions.

title: Practical Tomography of Multi-Time Processes
comment: 10 pages including supplementary. Version 1. Comments are welcome
summary: Characterising multi-time quantum processes is essential for analysing temporally correlated noise and for designing effective control and mitigation strategies. A complete operational description through multi-time process tomography requires an informationally complete set of probes, which necessarily includes non-deterministic intermediate operations. On present-day quantum devices, such operations are commonly implemented using mid-circuit measurements and reset, which are technologically limited and can introduce noise and overhead in terms of ancilla requirement. In this work, we study the minimal ancillary dimension required for complete characterisation of multi-time processes. We show that sequential interactions with a single qubit ancilla can generate an informationally complete family of correlated probes for processes of arbitrary length, without requiring mid-circuit measurements or reset. Our result provides a resource-efficient route for complete multi-time process tomography and establishes that one qubit of coherent ancillary memory suffices for full reconstruction of arbitrary multi-time dynamics.

AI.tldr: One qubit of coherent ancillary memory suffices for complete multi-time quantum process tomography without requiring mid-circuit measurements or reset operations.
AI.motivation: Current multi-time quantum process tomography requires non-deterministic intermediate operations implemented via mid-circuit measurements and reset, which introduce noise, overhead, and technological limitations. There's a need for resource-efficient characterization methods.
AI.method: Theoretical study of minimal ancillary dimension required for complete multi-time process characterization. Shows that sequential interactions with a single qubit ancilla can generate an informationally complete family of correlated probes for arbitrary-length processes, eliminating the need for mid-circuit measurements or reset operations.
AI.result: Demonstrates that one qubit of coherent ancillary memory is sufficient for full reconstruction of arbitrary multi-time quantum dynamics, providing a resource-efficient alternative to current tomography methods.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a fundamental resource bound for multi-time process tomography and provides a practical, resource-efficient route for complete characterization of temporally correlated quantum noise without the limitations of mid-circuit measurement-based approaches.

title: Resetting optimized competitive first-passage outcomes in non-Markovian systems
summary: We investigate the role of stochastic resetting in non-Markovian systems, where memory effects arise due to slow relaxation, rugged energy landscapes, disordered environments, and molecular crowding. Using the celebrated continuous-time random walk (CTRW) framework, we analyze first-passage processes with multiple competing outcomes and examine how resetting can selectively enhance desired events. We characterize the efficiency of resetting through conditional mean first-passage times (MFPTs) and demonstrate that its impact is highly sensitive to the underlying waiting-time statistics. Furthermore, we derive an inequality that quantifies how resetting controls fluctuations in conditional first-passage times (FPTs), revealing regimes where variability is significantly suppressed. Our results provide a systematic understanding of how long-term memory influences competitive first-passage outcomes and establish resetting as a powerful control mechanism beyond the conventional Markovian setting.

AI.tldr: Stochastic resetting in non-Markovian systems enhances selective outcomes in competitive first-passage processes by controlling memory effects and suppressing fluctuations.
AI.motivation: To understand how stochastic resetting influences competitive first-passage processes in non-Markovian systems with memory effects arising from slow relaxation, rugged energy landscapes, disordered environments, and molecular crowding.
AI.method: Using continuous-time random walk (CTRW) framework to analyze first-passage processes with multiple competing outcomes, characterizing efficiency through conditional mean first-passage times (MFPTs), and deriving an inequality quantifying how resetting controls fluctuations in conditional first-passage times.
AI.result: Resetting's impact is highly sensitive to underlying waiting-time statistics; it can selectively enhance desired events and significantly suppress variability in conditional first-passage times in certain regimes.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic resetting serves as a powerful control mechanism for competitive first-passage outcomes in non-Markovian systems, providing systematic understanding of how long-term memory influences these processes beyond conventional Markovian settings.

title: The Quantum Walk Characteristic Polynomial Distinguishes All Strongly Regular Graphs of Prime Orde
summary:
Let $G$ be a strongly regular graph of prime order $p$ with connection degree $k \geq 6$. We prove that the \emph{quantum walk characteristic polynomial} $χ_q(G,λ) \coloneqq \det(λI - U_G)$, where $U_G$ is the coined quantum walk operator on $G$, completely determines $G$ up to isomorphism within the class of strongly regular graphs of the same order.
  The proof proceeds in three steps. First, we show that $U_G$ block-diagonalizes under the discrete Fourier transform over $\Z_p$, yielding $p$ blocks $U_G^{(j)}$ of size $k \times k$. Second, we prove an explicit formula \[
  χ_q\!\bigl(U_G^{(j)}, λ\bigr) =
  (λ-1)^{(k-2)/2}(λ+1)^{(k-2)/2}
  \!\left(λ^2 - \tfrac{2\widehat{A}_G(j)}{k}\,λ+ 1\right), \] from which the Fourier coefficient $\widehat{A}_G(j)$ is recovered as the unique real part of an eigenvalue of $U_G^{(j)}$ distinct from $\pm 1$. Third, the inverse discrete Fourier transform recovers the connection set $S$ of $G$, and Turner's theorem (1967) identifies $G$ up to isomorphism. As a consequence, graph isomorphism is decidable in polynomial time within this class using the quantum walk spectrum, without resorting to the general quasi-polynomial algorithm of Babai (2016).

AI.tldr: Quantum walk characteristic polynomial uniquely determines strongly regular graphs of prime order with connection degree ≥6, enabling polynomial-time graph isomorphism decision within this class.
AI.motivation: To establish a polynomial-time algorithm for graph isomorphism within the class of strongly regular graphs of prime order, bypassing the need for Babai's quasi-polynomial general algorithm.
AI.method: Three-step approach: 1) Block-diagonalize quantum walk operator via discrete Fourier transform over ℤₚ, 2) Derive explicit formula for quantum walk characteristic polynomial to recover Fourier coefficients, 3) Reconstruct connection set via inverse Fourier transform and apply Turner's theorem for isomorphism identification.
AI.result: Quantum walk characteristic polynomial χ_q(G,λ) completely determines strongly regular graphs of prime order with k≥6 up to isomorphism, enabling polynomial-time graph isomorphism decision within this class.
AI.conclusion: Quantum walks provide an efficient spectral invariant for strongly regular graphs of prime order, offering a polynomial-time alternative to general graph isomorphism algorithms for this specific class.

title: Spatial Correlations Restore Zwanzig's Mean-Field Diffusion Result in Rugged Energy Landscapes
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2512.22015
summary: Transport in disordered environments is often controlled not by typical fluctuations but by rare, extreme events that dominate long-time dynamics. In such settings, Zwanzig's classic mean-field theory predicts that energetic roughness reduces the diffusion coefficient by an exponential factor governed solely by the variance of the disorder. However, this prediction breaks down in uncorrelated Gaussian landscapes, where rare but deep multi-site traps dominate transport and lead to a much stronger suppression of diffusion. Here, we present a unified theoretical framework that clarifies both the origin of this breakdown and its resolution. We show that Zwanzig's local averaging can be interpreted as a Gaussian cumulant expansion whose validity is destroyed by uncorrelated disorder through the emergence of extreme trapping events. Introducing Gaussian spatial correlations fundamentally reshapes the landscape: roughness increments become smoother, asymmetric multi-site traps are suppressed, and the statistics of escape pathways are regularized. As a result, Zwanzig's exponential scaling is recovered. We provide an explicit analytical derivation demonstrating how spatial correlations modify trap statistics and restore mean-field diffusion, complemented by illustrative numerical examples showing the dramatic reduction of escape times in correlated landscapes.

AI.tldr: Unified theory explains breakdown of Zwanzig's mean-field diffusion prediction in uncorrelated Gaussian disorder and shows how spatial correlations restore it by suppressing extreme multi-site traps.
AI.motivation: Zwanzig's classic mean-field theory predicts exponential reduction of diffusion coefficient based solely on disorder variance, but this breaks down in uncorrelated Gaussian landscapes where rare deep multi-site traps dominate transport.
AI.method: Theoretical framework interpreting Zwanzig's local averaging as Gaussian cumulant expansion, analyzing how uncorrelated disorder destroys validity through extreme trapping events, and demonstrating how Gaussian spatial correlations reshape landscape statistics.
AI.result: Spatial correlations suppress asymmetric multi-site traps, regularize escape pathway statistics, and restore Zwanzig's exponential scaling; analytical derivation shows how correlations modify trap statistics and numerical examples demonstrate dramatic reduction of escape times.
AI.conclusion: Breakdown of Zwanzig's prediction in uncorrelated disorder originates from extreme trapping events; introducing spatial correlations fundamentally reshapes landscape statistics, suppresses rare deep traps, and restores mean-field diffusion behavior.

title: Codimension-controlled universality of quantum Fisher information singularities at topological band-touching defects
summary: Topological phase transitions in generic multiband systems are mediated by band-touching defects whose codimension -- the number of momentum directions along which the gap closes linearly -- varies across universality classes. Although singular behavior of fidelity susceptibilities and quantum Fisher information (QFI) has been computed for specific models, no unifying principle connecting these results has been identified: it has remained unclear whether the controlling variable is spatial dimensionality, band structure, or an intrinsic geometric property of the defect. We resolve this question by showing that the singular contribution to the QFI with respect to the tuning parameter $m$ obeys a universal power-law scaling $\sim |m|^{p-2}$ for $p \neq 2$, with a logarithmic divergence $\sim \ln(1/|m|)$ at the marginal codimension $p = 2$, where $p$ denotes the codimension of the band-touching defect. This exponent is independent of spatial dimensionality, anisotropies, ultraviolet regularization, and additional gapped bands, and is protected by renormalization-group arguments at the linearized fixed point. The result unifies previously isolated observations for SSH chains ($p=1$), Chern insulators ($p=2$), and Weyl semimetals ($p=3$) as instances of a single codimension-dependent universality class, and reveals that only defects with $p \leq 2$ generate divergent information-geometric responses. This establishes a direct and previously missing link between topological classification in momentum space and quantum distinguishability in parameter space, with implications for metrological sensitivity near topological transitions and for the experimental detection of topological criticality via quantum geometric observables.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a universal scaling law for quantum Fisher information (QFI) near topological phase transitions, showing it scales as ∼|m|^{p-2} (logarithmic at p=2) where p is the codimension of band-touching defects, unifying various topological systems under a single codimension-dependent universality class.
AI.motivation: Previous studies computed singular behavior of fidelity susceptibilities and QFI for specific models, but lacked a unifying principle connecting these results. It was unclear whether the controlling variable was spatial dimensionality, band structure, or an intrinsic geometric property of the defect.
AI.method: The authors show that the singular contribution to QFI with respect to tuning parameter m obeys a universal power-law scaling ∼|m|^{p-2} for p≠2, with logarithmic divergence at p=2, where p is the codimension of band-touching defects. They use renormalization-group arguments at the linearized fixed point to demonstrate protection of this exponent.
AI.result: The exponent is independent of spatial dimensionality, anisotropies, ultraviolet regularization, and additional gapped bands. This unifies previously isolated observations for SSH chains (p=1), Chern insulators (p=2), and Weyl semimetals (p=3) as instances of a single codimension-dependent universality class. Only defects with p≤2 generate divergent information-geometric responses.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a direct link between topological classification in momentum space and quantum distinguishability in parameter space, with implications for metrological sensitivity near topological transitions and experimental detection of topological criticality via quantum geometric observables.

title: Power laws, anisotropy and center-of-mass conservation in mass transport processes
summary: We present exact results for steady-state density correlation functions in conserved-mass transport processes with {\it anisotropic}, reflection-symmetric hopping on a $d-$dimensional hypercubic lattice. In addition to mass conservation, we consider center-of-mass (CoM) conservation, imposed either along a specific axis or along all axes. CoM-conserving dynamics is implemented through coordinated {\it multidirectional} hopping of two equal chunks of masses in {\it opposite} directions. While anisotropy and mass conservation are known to generate power-law density correlations $C({\bf x}) \sim 1/|{\bf x}|^d$ at large distance $|{\bf x}| \gg 1$ {\it [Phys. Rev. A {\bf 42}, 1954 (1990)]}, an additional CoM conservation can qualitatively alter the nature of the power law. Indeed, when CoM is conserved in {\it all} directions, the correlations decay faster $-$ typically as $C({\bf x}) \sim 1/|{\bf x}|^{(d+2)}$, regardless of the presence (or absence) of anisotropy. Consequently, the systems exhibit an extreme {\it hyperuniformity} (``class I''), where the long-wavelength density fluctuations, despite the slow power-law decay, are anomalously suppressed. When CoM is conserved along particular ({\it not} all) directions, the slower $1/|{\bf x}|^{d}$ power-law decay is recovered. The above behavior can be understood from an analogy between the correlation function and an electrostatic potential: While a (rank-$2$) quadrupolar charge distribution gives rise to the $1/|{\bf x}|^{d}$ power law, the $1/|{\bf x}|^{(d+2)}$ power law originates from a higher-order (rank-$4$) multipolar charge distribution. These findings reveal a rich interplay between anisotropy and CoM conservation in nonequilibrium steady states.

AI.tldr: Exact results show that anisotropic mass transport with center-of-mass conservation generates different power-law density correlations: 1/|x|^d when CoM conserved along specific axes, and faster 1/|x|^(d+2) decay when CoM conserved in all directions, leading to extreme hyperuniformity.
AI.motivation: To understand how anisotropy combined with different types of center-of-mass conservation affects density correlations in nonequilibrium steady states of conserved-mass transport processes, particularly how these constraints qualitatively alter power-law decay behavior.
AI.method: Exact analytical calculation of steady-state density correlation functions for anisotropic, reflection-symmetric hopping on d-dimensional hypercubic lattice with mass conservation and two types of CoM conservation: along specific axes or all axes, implemented through coordinated multidirectional hopping of two equal mass chunks in opposite directions.
AI.result: When CoM conserved in all directions, correlations decay as C(x) ∼ 1/|x|^(d+2) regardless of anisotropy, exhibiting extreme hyperuniformity (class I). When CoM conserved only along particular directions, slower 1/|x|^d decay is recovered. The behavior is explained via electrostatic analogy: 1/|x|^d corresponds to rank-2 quadrupolar charge distribution, while 1/|x|^(d+2) corresponds to rank-4 multipolar distribution.
AI.conclusion: Anisotropy and CoM conservation exhibit rich interplay in nonequilibrium steady states, with CoM conservation in all directions qualitatively altering correlation decay to faster 1/|x|^(d+2) power law and inducing extreme hyperuniformity, while partial CoM conservation yields the standard 1/|x|^d decay.

title: DQC1-completeness of normalized trace estimation for functions of log-local Hamiltonians
summary:
We study the computational complexity of estimating the normalized trace $2^{-n}Tr[f(A)]$ for a log-local Hamiltonian $A$ acting on $n$ qubits. This problem arises naturally in the DQC1 model, yet its complexity is only understood for a limited class of functions $f(x)$.
  We show that if $f(x)$ is a continuous function with approximate degree $Ω({\rm poly}(n))$, then estimating $2^{-n}Tr[f(A)]$ up to constant additive error is DQC1-complete, under a technical condition on the polynomial approximation error of $f(x)$. This condition holds for a broad class of functions, including exponentials, trigonometric functions, logarithms, and inverse-type functions. We further prove that when $A$ is sparse, the classical query complexity of this problem is exponential in the approximate degree, assuming a conjectured lower bound for a trace variant of the $k$-Forrelation problem in the DQC1 query model. Together, these results identify the approximate degree as the key parameter governing the complexity of normalized trace estimation: it characterizes both the quantum complexity (via efficient DQC1 algorithms) and, conditionally, the classical hardness, yielding an exponential quantum-classical separation. Our proof develops a unified framework that cleanly combines circuit-to-Hamiltonian constructions, periodic Jacobi operators, and tools from polynomial approximation theory, including the Chebyshev equioscillation theorem.

AI.tldr: Normalized trace estimation for log-local Hamiltonians is DQC1-complete when f has high approximate degree, with exponential quantum-classical separation.
AI.motivation: The problem arises naturally in the DQC1 model, but its computational complexity is only understood for limited function classes. There's a need to characterize when this problem is classically hard versus quantumly easy.
AI.method: Develops a unified framework combining circuit-to-Hamiltonian constructions, periodic Jacobi operators, and polynomial approximation theory tools including the Chebyshev equioscillation theorem. Uses approximate degree as the key parameter.
AI.result: Shows that for continuous functions with Ω(poly(n)) approximate degree, normalized trace estimation is DQC1-complete. For sparse Hamiltonians, classical query complexity is exponential in approximate degree, yielding exponential quantum-classical separation.
AI.conclusion: Approximate degree is the key parameter governing normalized trace estimation complexity: it characterizes quantum efficiency (DQC1 algorithms) and, conditionally, classical hardness, establishing exponential quantum-classical separation.

title: Bootstrapping Symmetries in Quantum Many-Body Systems from the Cross Spectral Form Factor
summary: Symmetries play a central role in quantum many-body physics, yet uncovering them systematically remains challenging. We introduce a bootstrap framework designed to reconstruct the representation theory of hidden finite group symmetries of quantum many-body lattice Hamiltonians, using only a known symmetry subgroup $N$ and spectral correlations between its symmetry sectors. We introduce a novel variant of the spectral form factor, the cross spectral form factor (xSFF), which we compute via exact diagonalization to seed the bootstrap algorithm. By applying the constraints derived from these data alongside the algebraic conditions of the fusion rules, our bootstrap procedure sharply restricts the set of candidate groups $G$. Remarkably, without any prior assumptions regarding the full symmetry group $G$, our method can systematically recover its representation-theoretic data, including the number and dimensions of the irreducible representations, their branching rules with respect to $N$, the fusion algebra, and the full character table. This framework applies equally well to chaotic and integrable many-body systems and accommodates both unitary and anti-unitary symmetries. Through various examples, we demonstrate that the underlying group $G$ can be uniquely identified. In particular, our bootstrap independently recovers the $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry at the self-dual point of the three-state quantum torus chain, detects signatures of projective representations in the effective Hamiltonian of the driven Bose-Hubbard model, and rediscovers the $η$-pairing $\mathrm{SO}(4)$ symmetry of the one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model. Our framework thus establishes a practical route to identify symmetries directly from dynamical spectral observables.

AI.tldr: A bootstrap framework reconstructs hidden finite group symmetries in quantum many-body systems using spectral correlations and known subgroup symmetries.
AI.motivation: Symmetries are fundamental in quantum many-body physics but systematic methods to uncover hidden symmetries remain challenging, especially when only partial symmetry information is available.
AI.method: Introduces a bootstrap framework using cross spectral form factor (xSFF) computed via exact diagonalization, combined with algebraic constraints from fusion rules to reconstruct representation theory of hidden symmetries.
AI.result: The method can uniquely identify underlying symmetry groups, recover irreducible representation data, branching rules, fusion algebra, and character tables for both chaotic and integrable systems with unitary/anti-unitary symmetries.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a practical route to identify symmetries directly from dynamical spectral observables, enabling systematic discovery of hidden symmetries in quantum many-body systems.

title: Scalable Ground-State Certification of Quantum Spin Systems via Structured Noncommutative Polynomial Optimization
summary: A fundamental challenge in quantum physics is determining the ground-state properties of many-body systems. Whereas standard approaches, such as variational calculations, consist of writing down a wave function ansatz and minimizing over the possible states expressible by this ansatz, one can alternatively formulate the problem as a noncommutative polynomial optimization problem. This optimization problem can then be addressed using a hierarchy of semidefinite programming relaxations. In contrast to variational calculations, the semidefinite program can provide lower bounds for ground state energies and upper and lower bounds on observable expectation values. However, this approach typically suffers from severe scalability issues, limiting its applicability to small-to-medium-scale systems. In this article, we demonstrate that leveraging the inherent structures of the system can significantly mitigate these scalability challenges and thus allows us to compute meaningful bounds for quantum spin systems on up to $16\times16$ square lattices.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a scalable approach using semidefinite programming relaxations to compute bounds for ground-state properties of quantum spin systems by leveraging inherent system structures.
AI.motivation: Standard variational methods for ground-state properties of many-body quantum systems are limited to upper bounds, while semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations can provide both lower bounds for energies and bounds on observables, but suffer from severe scalability issues that restrict applicability to small systems.
AI.method: Formulates ground-state determination as a noncommutative polynomial optimization problem addressed through a hierarchy of semidefinite programming relaxations, with key innovation being exploitation of inherent system structures (symmetries, locality, tensor network structure) to dramatically reduce computational complexity.
AI.result: The structural exploitation enables computation of meaningful bounds for quantum spin systems on up to 16×16 square lattices, representing a significant scalability improvement over previous SDP approaches limited to small-to-medium systems.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging inherent structures of quantum systems can effectively mitigate scalability challenges in semidefinite programming approaches, enabling practical computation of rigorous bounds for ground-state properties of large-scale quantum spin systems previously inaccessible to such methods.

title: Quantum-Enhanced Processing with Tensor-Network Frontends for Privacy-Aware Federated Medical Diagnosis
comment: Submitted to the IEEE International Conference on Quantum Computing and Engineering 2026
summary: We propose a privacy-aware hybrid framework for federated medical image classification that combines tensor-network representation learning, MPC-secured aggregation, and post-aggregation quantum refinement. The framework is motivated by two practical constraints in privacy-aware federated learning: MPC can introduce substantial communication overhead, and direct quantum processing of high-dimensional medical images is unrealistic with a small number of qubits. To address both constraints within a single architecture, client-side tensor-network frontends, Matrix Product State (MPS), Tree Tensor Network (TTN), and Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA), compress local inputs into compact latent representations, after which a Quantum-Enhanced Processor (QEP) refines the aggregated latent feature through quantum-state embedding and observable-based readout. Experiments on PneumoniaMNIST show that the effect of the QEP is frontend-dependent rather than uniform across architectures. In the present setting, the TTN+QEP combination exhibits the most balanced overall profile. The results also suggest that the QEP behaves more stably when the qubit count is sufficiently matched to the latent dimension, while noisy conditions degrade performance relative to the noiseless setting. The MPC benchmark further shows that communication cost is governed primarily by the dimension of the protected latent representation. This indicates that tensor-network compression plays a dual role: it enables small-qubit quantum processing on compressed latent features and reduces the communication overhead associated with secure aggregation. Taken together, these results support a co-design perspective in which representation compression, post-aggregation quantum refinement, and privacy-aware deployment should be optimized jointly.

AI.tldr: A privacy-aware federated learning framework for medical image classification combining tensor-network compression, MPC-secured aggregation, and quantum refinement to address communication overhead and qubit limitations.
AI.motivation: Address two practical constraints in privacy-aware federated learning: 1) MPC introduces substantial communication overhead, and 2) direct quantum processing of high-dimensional medical images is unrealistic with limited qubits.
AI.method: Hybrid framework with client-side tensor-network frontends (MPS, TTN, MERA) compressing local inputs into compact latent representations, MPC-secured aggregation of these representations, and post-aggregation quantum refinement via Quantum-Enhanced Processor (QEP) with quantum-state embedding and observable-based readout.
AI.result: QEP effect is frontend-dependent (not uniform), TTN+QEP shows most balanced profile, QEP stability requires qubit count matched to latent dimension, noisy conditions degrade performance, MPC communication cost governed by latent dimension size, tensor-network compression enables dual benefits: small-qubit quantum processing and reduced communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: Supports co-design perspective where representation compression, post-aggregation quantum refinement, and privacy-aware deployment should be jointly optimized for effective privacy-aware federated medical image classification.

title: Mechanism for scale-free skin effect in one-dimensional systems
summary: Non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) is one of the most fascinating phenomena in non-Hermitian systems, which refers to enormous eigenstates localize at the boundary exponentially under open boundary condition (OBC). For typical NHSE, the localization length for a skin mode is independent of the system's size. Recently, some studies have revealed that for specific $1$-dimensional model, the localization length for eigenstates are proportional to the system's length under generalized boundary condition (GBC), and such phenomenon is dubbed as scale-free skin effect (SFSE). Further, SFSE is discovered in $1$-dimensional Hermitian chain with pure imaginary impurity at the end. In this work, we propose a mechanism for SFSE in 1-dimensional systems, which is model-independent. Our work provide a viewpoint for researching SFSE and shed new light on understanding finite size effect in non-Hermitian systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-independent mechanism for scale-free skin effect (SFSE) in 1D systems, where localization length scales with system size, contrasting with typical NHSE where localization is size-independent.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental mechanism behind scale-free skin effect (SFSE) in 1D systems, which differs from typical non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) by having localization length proportional to system size rather than being size-independent.
AI.method: Develops a model-independent theoretical framework for SFSE in 1-dimensional systems, analyzing the phenomenon under generalized boundary conditions rather than being tied to specific models.
AI.result: Provides a general mechanism explaining SFSE that applies across different models, offering new insights into finite size effects in non-Hermitian systems and connecting previously observed SFSE phenomena in various contexts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model-independent mechanism offers a unified perspective for understanding scale-free skin effect and advances the theoretical understanding of finite size effects in non-Hermitian systems.

title: A Differentiable Physical Framework for Goal-Driven Spin-State Engineering in Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy
summary: Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (MRS) offers a unique non-invasive window into metabolic processes, yet its potential remains strictly constrained by severe spectral congestion and intrinsic insensitivity. Traditional pulse sequence design, tethered to human intuition, predominantly targets simple quantum states, thereby overlooking the vast majority of the exponentially scaling operator space which consists of complex spin superpositions. Here, we introduce a spectrum-driven, end-to-end differentiable physical framework that transcends these heuristic limitations. By integrating physical laws with automatic differentiation algorithm, our approach directly navigates the high-dimensional spin dynamics space, bypassing the intractable inverse problem of state preparation. This enables the discovery of non-intuitive, complex mixed states that simultaneously satisfy the dual objectives of selective excitation and interferometric signal enhancement. We validate this paradigm by achieving the robust separation of Glutamate and Glutamine, which is a longstanding neuroimaging challenge, in the human brain at 3T, demonstrating spectral fidelity superior to conventional methods. By unlocking the "dark" informational content of nuclear spin ensembles, our work establishes a generalizable paradigm for goal-driven quantum state engineering in magnetic resonance and beyond.

AI.tldr: A differentiable physical framework for automated design of MRS pulse sequences that discovers complex quantum states for selective metabolite excitation and signal enhancement, validated by separating Glutamate and Glutamine at 3T.
AI.motivation: Traditional MRS pulse sequence design is limited by human intuition, focusing on simple quantum states while ignoring the vast majority of complex spin superpositions in the exponentially scaling operator space, constraining spectral resolution and sensitivity.
AI.method: End-to-end differentiable physical framework integrating physical laws with automatic differentiation to directly navigate high-dimensional spin dynamics space, bypassing the intractable inverse problem of state preparation.
AI.result: Achieved robust separation of Glutamate and Glutamine in human brain at 3T, demonstrating superior spectral fidelity compared to conventional methods by discovering non-intuitive complex mixed states.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a generalizable paradigm for goal-driven quantum state engineering in magnetic resonance by unlocking the "dark" informational content of nuclear spin ensembles through automated discovery of optimal quantum states.

title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
summary: We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.

AI.tldr: General scheme for distributing pairwise entanglement in quantum networks using environmental ancilla qubits and tunable Hamiltonians, analyzed through collision and repeated interaction models.
AI.motivation: To develop a systematic and physically implementable method for controllable entanglement distribution in quantum networks, addressing the challenge of generating entanglement between nodes that may not directly interact.
AI.method: Uses environmental ancilla qubits interacting with network nodes via tunable Hamiltonians within collision model frameworks. Two scenarios: traditional collision model (ancilla reset after each interaction) and repeated interaction model (ancilla state carried over). System-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. Interaction patterns varied to enable selective entanglement generation.
AI.result: Demonstrates selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes without direct interactions. In networks up to three qubits, achieves generation of maximally entangled Bell pairs even when interacting ancilla couples to only a single node.
AI.conclusion: Provides a systematic, physically implementable route to entanglement distribution with potential applications in quantum communication, metrology, and modular quantum computing.

title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
summary: Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required.
